# How much data, and whose {#sec-data-whose}

The previous two chapters are a negative: alignment cannot be self-discovered from
co-occurrence (@sec-bootstrap), and the social cues that might supply it are, at 1 fps, too
weak to read (@sec-social). That invites a step back. Our natural numbers sit around 35–50
4AFC; a clean-label topline reaches ~72; chance is 25. *Why* are the numbers where they are —
and is the pipeline even competitive with published work on similar data? @vonglake2026, a
close analogue of our setup (a frozen ViT plus a bag-of-words language encoder, trained on
automatically transcribed child speech), lets us answer this by elimination, because it
ablates most of the usual suspects on the SAYCam corpus.

## What is *not* the problem

Four candidate explanations for a low number can be ruled out before running anything of our
own, from @vonglake2026's ablations:

- **Automatic transcription is fine.** Whisper transcripts reach 56.9% versus 61.6% for
  manual — a few points, not a chasm. Our Whisper pipeline is not the leak.
- **A better language model does not help.** A bag-of-words encoder, a GPT-2 transformer, and
  a transformer with an added language-modelling loss all land within a point or two. The
  classification eval measures vision–language *alignment*, which every language encoder
  optimises equally.
- **More data plateaus quickly.** 3.5× the training data buys ~6 points.
- **What matters is the absolute *count* of well-aligned pairs, not their average quality**
  (their Finding 5): across children, the number of CLIP-aligned pairs — not the mean CLIP
  similarity — tracks accuracy.

And the eval itself caps low: a strong CLIP ViT-L/14 scores only 66.7% zero-shot on their
labelled test, failing on ambiguous categories like *floor* and *hand*. So the achievable
ceiling on a looking-while-listening eval of naturalistic frames is ~70%, not 100% — our ~72
topline is, if anything, strong.

## We are not pair-starved

Finding 5 makes pair count the thing to check first, and here BabyView is comfortable. Tracing
the funnel for one well-resourced child (S00510002): 100.5k Whisper utterances → 119.4k
CLIP-scored → 113.0k with a region embedding (only 5% lost in our pipeline) → **18,683 aligned
pairs** at clip > 0.24 (a 15.6% alignment rate). The comparable single child in @vonglake2026
(baby S) has ~9,320 aligned pairs at a 7.6% rate. **Our single child has twice the aligned
data, at twice the alignment rate, and we barely lose any of it downstream.** Whatever explains
our numbers, it is not a shortage of aligned pairs.

## Within-child is harder, not easier

@vonglake2026 evaluate *within* child — train on baby S, test on held-out frames of baby S's
own videos. Every result in this book, by contrast, holds out an entire child. If a child's
consistent world is what makes CVCL work, our cross-child setup should be strictly harder, and
adding a within-child comparison should raise the number. We built one: for each of the three
best-resourced children, split *videos* (not frames — frames within a video are contiguous and
would leak) into an earlier-80% train and a latest-20% test, train region-MIL on the child's
own pairs, and evaluate on a 4AFC built from CDI detections in that child's held-out videos,
matched to the same categories as the cross-child eval.

| child (within-child oracle) | aligned train pairs | 4AFC |
|-----------------------------|---------------------|------|
| S00510002 | 15.0k | 33.1 |
| S00320001 | 10.6k | 29.7 |
| S00400001 | 10.6k | 36.1 |

The result is the opposite of the prediction: within-child sits at **30–36**, *well below* the
cross-child pooled oracle (~48–50). Training on a single child's own data — even to predict
that same child's held-out videos — is worse than training on the pool. (The oracle filter
barely helps within a single child, because with only ~10–15k aligned pairs there is too
little for it to concentrate.)

## Diversity, not just count

Why is a single child worse, when Finding 5 says count is king and this child has *more*
aligned pairs than @vonglake2026's? Because two things are conflated — how *much* aligned data,
and *whose*. We can separate them by matching the count. Training a pooled model on exactly the
same 15k aligned pairs as the single child, and evaluating all models on the *same*
(S00510002) held-out eval:

| training set | aligned pairs | 4AFC |
|--------------|---------------|------|
| single child (S00510002) | 15k | 33.1 |
| **pooled (many children)** | **15k** | **38.3** |
| pooled (many children) | 110k | 41.2 |

At *identical* count, **pooling across children beats a single child by +5.2** — that is
diversity, not amount. Count then adds a further +2.9 (15k → 110k, with diminishing returns).
So the single-child deficit is roughly **two-thirds diversity, one-third count**. A pool that
has seen *chair*, *cup*, and *book* across many homes builds a concept that generalises — even
to a single child's own held-out chairs — better than that child's own repeated exposure does.
The consistency of one child's world, which gave the bootstrap its only faint toehold
(@sec-e0), actively *hurts* generalisable word learning. **Whose** the data is matters more
than how much of it there is.

## The gap that wasn't

This also dissolves the apparent gap to @vonglake2026. Their within-child model reaches ~51;
our *within-child* models reach ~33 — but that comparison pits our data-starved single child
against their setup. Our *pooled* oracle reaches ~48–50, which sits right beside their 51.
Allowing for the different evals and architectures, there is no large unexplained gap: the
supervised, pooled pipeline is competitive with published work on comparable data.

A final check confirms the eval is not secretly the culprit. Restricting the eval to a curated
subset of 45 unambiguous concrete-object categories — dropping places (*kitchen*), body parts
(*hand*), polysemes (*can*, *present*), and superordinates (*toy*) on linguistic grounds — does
*not* raise accuracy (pooled 48.1 → 48.6; within-child 33.1 → 31.2). In a 4-alternative task a
hard category is also an easy distractor, so pruning the hard ones cancels out. The number is
robust to which words are on the test.

## Synthesis

Set against @vonglake2026, our numbers are where they should be, and the low spots are
accounted for, not mysterious.

- The pipeline is **not pair-starved** (twice the aligned data of a comparable single child)
  and **not eval-limited by category difficulty** (robust to curation); the **pooled
  supervised model is competitive** with published results.
- The genuinely low numbers trace to two things, both established rather than broken. First,
  the **bootstrap does not ignite** (@sec-bootstrap, @sec-social): alignment cannot be
  self-discovered here. Second, a **single child is data-limited** — and not only in amount:
  the larger cost is a **lack of diversity**, because generalisable word learning is driven by
  seeing each thing across many contexts.

The developmental reading is worth stating plainly. A model of *one* child's experience is the
natural target for a theory of how *that* child learns — but it inherits that child's narrow
slice of the world, and a narrow slice generalises poorly. Pooling across children is not
biologically faithful, yet it is what buys the diversity that makes word–object mappings
transfer. The tension between fidelity to a single developmental stream and the diversity that
learning seems to need is, we think, the more interesting question these experiments leave
open — and the one a corpus like BabyView, with many children each recorded at length, is
built to study.
